title: Risk-Reward Ratio Calculator for Trading | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your risk-reward ratio instantly. Find minimum win rate, potential profit/loss, and verify if your trade meets professional standards. Free R:R…
intro: The **Risk/Reward Ratio (R:R)** is the single most important metric for deciding whether a trade is worth taking. It compares how much you're willing to risk (distance to your stop-loss) against how much you stand to gain (distance to your take-profit). An **R:R of 1:2** means you risk $1 to make $2 — with just a 40% win rate, you're already profitable. This calculator takes your entry price, stop-loss, and take-profit and returns: your exact R:R, the minimum win rate needed to break even, absolute profit and loss amounts, and whether your trade meets the professional standard (**minimum 1:2**). Works with forex, stocks, crypto, futures, and options.
keyTakeaway: **R:R = (TP − Entry) / (Entry − SL)**. Professional minimum: **1:2**. Break-even win rate = 1/(1+R).
useCases: Filter trades before entering: if R:R < 1:2, skip it.
Compare two setups and choose the one with the better ratio.
Verify your strategy has positive mathematical expectancy.
Justify wider stops with even wider profit targets.
Teach new traders how edge and probability work together.

fields.label: Stop-Loss
fields.help: Price level at which you exit if the trade moves against you.

fields.label: Position Size (units)
fields.help: Number of units, contracts, or shares you will trade.

example.title: Entry $100, SL $95, TP $115, 100 units
example.result: R:R **1:3** — excellent. Win just 1 out of every 4 trades and you break even.

explanation:
## The Math Behind Edge

The secret to profitable trading is simple: **positive expectancy**.

```
Expectancy = (Win Rate × Avg Win) − (Loss Rate × Avg Loss)
```

A trader with a 1:3 R:R needs only a **25% win rate** to break even. A 1:1 R:R trader needs **>50%**. That's why professionals hunt asymmetric trades.

## Win Rate Requirements by R:R

| R:R | Min. Win Rate | Safety Margin |
|---|---|---|
| 1:1 | 50% | too tight |
| 1:1.5 | 40% | acceptable |
| 1:2 | 33% | ✅ pro standard |
| 1:3 | 25% | excellent |
| 1:5 | 17% | swing long-term |
| 1:10 | 9% | home-run only |

## Formulas

```
R:R = (TP − Entry) / (Entry − Stop)
Break-even Win Rate = 1 / (1 + R)
Expectancy = p×W − (1−p)×L
```

## Common Mistakes

1. **Moving your target higher after entry**: Destroys your R:R. Lock it in before you trade.
2. **Widening your stop loss**: Increases risk without improving reward. That's worse, not better.
3. **Calculating R:R after entering**: Too late. R:R is a pre-entry decision.

Related tools: [Position Size](/position-size-forex-pips-risk) · [Kelly Criterion](/kelly-criterion-optimal-bet) · [Break-Even](/breakeven-trade-commissions)

> ⚠️ **Educational calculator only.** Not financial advice. Markets risk total loss. Consult a licensed advisor before trading.

faq.a: 1:2 is the professional standard. Some traders demand 1:3. Below 1:1.5, you need a win rate above 55% to stay profitable—very difficult to maintain consistently.

faq.q: Does a high R:R guarantee I'll make money?
faq.a: No. A good R:R gives you *mathematical edge*, but only if you achieve the required win rate. A 1:5 ratio with a 10% win rate still loses money. You need both: good R:R *and* execution.

faq.q: How do I calculate risk-reward ratio?
faq.a: **R:R = (Take-Profit − Entry) / (Entry − Stop-Loss)**. Divide your potential reward by your potential risk. The result is your ratio (e.g., 1:3).

faq.q: Is risk-reward ratio the same for all markets?
faq.a: Yes. R:R is market-agnostic—it only depends on your three prices: entry, stop-loss, and take-profit. The math works identically for forex, stocks, crypto, futures, and options.

faq.q: What's the minimum win rate I need to break even?
faq.a: Use: **Win Rate = 1 / (1 + R)**. For 1:2, that's 33%. For 1:3, that's 25%. For 1:1, that's 50%. This is your break-even threshold.

faq.q: How do trading commissions affect my R:R?
faq.a: Subtract them from your reward, add them to your risk. On crypto with 0.1% maker + 0.1% taker fees, a 1:2 gross ratio becomes roughly 1:1.8 net. Always plan on *net* R:R, not gross.

faq.q: How do I use R:R with position sizing?
faq.a: First: decide R:R (is this trade good?). Second: choose position size (how much risk?). Reverse that order and emotion takes over. R:R is quality; position size is quantity.

faq.q: Can I fix a bad R:R by moving my stop closer?
faq.a: No—that makes it worse. Tighter stops increase risk without improving reward. Bad R:R means skip the trade, not squeeze your stops.

sources.name: Van Tharp - Definitive Guide to Position Sizing
Mark Douglas - Trading in the Zone
